title: Do not hide navigation behind hamburger on desktop viewports
summary: AI-generated interfaces default to hamburger menus on desktop, hiding navigation and reducing discoverability.
instruction: Show primary navigation links visibly in the header on viewports wider than 768px. Reserve hamburger menus for narrow viewports.
rationale: Hamburger menus reduce discoverability by hiding links. On desktop viewports with horizontal space, visible navigation improves engagement and task completion.

violations: A marketing page at 1440px with a hamburger icon hiding all navigation links.
A dashboard at 1920px with hamburger for 4 primary sections that would fit in the header.
controls: A marketing page with visible nav links at desktop, collapsing to hamburger below 768px.
A dashboard with persistent left sidebar showing section names at desktop.
borderline: Apps with 7+ navigation items may need to prioritize or combine visible top-level with hamburger for secondary.

exceptions.description: Content-forward pages where the entire viewport is for reading. Minimalist portfolio or artistic sites with documented design direction.

detector.signalDescription: Detect hamburger icon (three-line SVG or unicode) as primary navigation on desktop-width viewports (>768px).
limitations: JavaScript toggling may not appear in static CSS analysis.

scenarios: Apps with only 1-2 pages where hamburger containing just Home and About is fine.
Mobile-first PWAs using mobile patterns at all widths intentionally.
mitigations: Require at least 3 nav items to flag. Check breakpoint in responsive CSS.

fixturePlan.description: A 1440px landing page with hamburger icon hiding 6 nav links.
Same page with visible nav links at 1440px, hamburger below 768px.
A portfolio with hamburger at all widths but only 2 nav items, documented design direction.
